sn35.238:1.1: I have heard that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove. Then he addressed the monks, “Monks, suppose there were four vipers of utmost heat & horrible venom.
sn35.238:1.2: Then a man would come along—desiring life, desiring not to die, desiring happiness, & loathing pain—
sn35.238:1.4: ‘Good man, these four vipers, of utmost heat & horrible venom, are yours. Time after time they must be lifted up, time after time they must be bathed, time after time they must be fed, time after time put to rest.
sn35.238:1.5: And if any of these vipers ever gets provoked1 with you, then you will meet with death or death-like suffering.
sn35.238:1.6: Do what you think should be done.’
sn35.238:2.1: “Then the man—afraid of the four vipers of utmost heat & horrible venom—would flee this way or that.
sn35.238:2.3: ‘Good man, there are five enemy executioners chasing right on your heels, (thinking,) “Wherever we see him, we’ll kill him right on the spot.”
sn35.238:2.4: Do what you think should be done.’
sn35.238:3.1: “Then the man—afraid of the four vipers of utmost heat & horrible venom, afraid of the five enemy executioners—would flee this way or that.
sn35.238:3.3: ‘Good man, there is a sixth executioner, a fellow-traveler, chasing right on your heels with upraised sword, (thinking,) “Wherever I see him, I’ll kill him right on the spot.”
sn35.238:3.4: Do what you think should be done.’
sn35.238:4.1: “Then the man—afraid of the four vipers of utmost heat & horrible venom, afraid of the five enemy executioners, afraid of the sixth fellow-traveling executioner with upraised sword—would flee this way or that.
sn35.238:4.2: He would see an empty village.
sn35.238:4.3: Whatever house he entered would be abandoned, void, & empty as he entered it.
sn35.238:4.4: Whatever pot he grabbed hold of would be abandoned, void, & empty as he grabbed hold of it.
sn35.238:4.6: ‘Good man, right now, village-plundering bandits are entering this empty village.
sn35.238:4.7: Do what you think should be done.’
sn35.238:5.1: “Then the man—afraid of the four vipers of utmost heat & horrible venom, afraid of the five enemy executioners, afraid of the sixth fellow-traveling executioner with upraised sword, afraid of the village-plundering bandits—would flee this way or that.
sn35.238:5.2: He would see a great expanse of water, with the near shore dubious & risky, the further shore secure & free from risk,
sn35.238:5.3: but with neither a ferryboat nor a bridge going from this shore to the other.
sn35.238:5.4: The thought would occur to him,
sn35.238:5.5: ‘Here is this great expanse of water, with the near shore dubious & risky, the further shore secure & free from risk, but with neither a ferryboat nor a bridge going from this shore to the other.
sn35.238:5.6: What if I were to gather grass, twigs, branches, & leaves and, having bound them together to make a raft, were to cross over to safety on the other shore in dependence on the raft, making an effort with my hands & feet?’
sn35.238:6.1: Then the man, having gathered grass, twigs, branches, & leaves, having bound them together to make a raft, would cross over to safety on the other shore in dependence on the raft, making an effort with his hands & feet. Crossed over, having gone to the other shore, he would stand on high ground, a brahman.
sn35.238:7.1: “Monks, I have made this simile to convey a meaning.
sn35.238:7.3: ‘The four vipers of utmost heat & horrible venom’ stands for the four great elements:
sn35.238:7.4: the earth property, the liquid property, the fire-property, & the wind property.
sn35.238:8.1: ‘The five enemy executioners’ stands for the five clinging-aggregates:
sn35.238:8.2: the form clinging-aggregate, the feeling clinging-aggregate, the perception clinging-aggregate, the fabrications clinging-aggregate, the consciousness clinging-aggregate.
sn35.238:9.1: ‘The sixth fellow-traveling executioner with upraised sword’ stands for passion & delight.
sn35.238:10.1: “‘The empty village’ stands for the six internal sense media.
sn35.238:10.2: If a wise, competent, intelligent person examines them from the point of view of the eye, they appear abandoned, void, & empty. If he examines them from the point of view of the ear… the nose…
sn35.238:10.4: the intellect, they appear abandoned, void, & empty.
sn35.238:11.1: ‘The village-plundering bandits’ stands for the six external sense-media.
sn35.238:11.2: The eye is attacked by agreeable & disagreeable forms.
sn35.238:11.3: The ear is attacked by agreeable & disagreeable sounds.
sn35.238:11.4: The nose is attacked by agreeable & disagreeable aromas.
sn35.238:11.5: The tongue is attacked by agreeable & disagreeable flavors.
sn35.238:11.6: The body is attacked by agreeable & disagreeable tactile sensations.
sn35.238:11.7: The intellect is attacked by agreeable & disagreeable ideas.
sn35.238:12.1: “‘The great expanse of water’ stands for the fourfold flood:
sn35.238:12.2: the flood of sensuality, the flood of becoming, the flood of views, & the flood of ignorance.
sn35.238:13.1: ‘The near shore, dubious & risky’ stands for self-identification.
sn35.238:14.1: ‘The further shore, secure and free from risk’ stands for unbinding.
sn35.238:15.1: ‘The raft’ stands for just this noble eightfold path:
sn35.238:15.2: right view, right resolve, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration.
sn35.238:16.1: ‘Making an effort with hands & feet’ stands for the arousing of persistence.
sn35.238:17.1: ‘Crossed over, having gone to the other shore, he would stand on high ground, a brahman’ stands for the arahant.”
